title: Handling Medical & Legal Emergencies
learningGoal: The learner will master the vocabulary and grammatical structures necessary to report symptoms to a doctor, report accidents/crimes to the police, and ask for emergency help.
instructions: In an emergency, you need to communicate clearly and quickly. This lesson focuses on medical vocabulary, reporting lost or stolen items, and explaining unfortunate events using the '~te shimaimashita' (unfortunately happened) structure.

lessonContent.bodyText: To explain pain, use '[Body Part] ga itai desu'. For other symptoms, use specific verbs like 'deru' (to come out) or 'hiku' (to catch).

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: あたまが いたいです。
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: My head hurts. (I have a headache).

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: ねつが あります。そして、せきが でます。
targetLanguageExamples.transliteration: Netsu ga arimasu. Soshite, seki ga demasu.
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: I have a fever. And, I have a cough (a cough comes out).

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: かぜを ひきました。

lessonContent.bodyText: When you want to emphasize that something happened accidentally or unfortunately, add 'shimaimashita' to the Te-form of the verb.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: さいふを なくして しまいました。
かいだんで ころんで しまいました。
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: I (unfortunately) fell down on the stairs.

lessonContent.sectionTitle: At the Police Box (Kouban): Reporting Crimes
lessonContent.bodyText: When reporting a crime, you will often use the Passive Voice, as you are the victim of an action.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: パスポートを ぬすまれました。
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: My passport was stolen. (I had my passport stolen).

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: どろぼうに はいられました。
targetLanguageExamples.baseTranslation: My house was broken into by a thief. (Literally: I was entered by a thief).

lessonContent.bodyText: In a severe emergency, use 'Te-form + kudasai' with strong verbs to demand immediate assistance.

targetLanguageExamples.targetText: きゅうきゅうしゃを よんで ください！
けいさつを よんで ください！

learningObjective: The learner will be able to describe medical symptoms accurately, report lost or stolen items using the passive voice, and request emergency services using command structures.

examples.targetText: おなかが いたいです。くすりを ください。
examples.transliteration: Onaka ga itai desu. Kusuri wo kudasai.
examples.baseTranslation: My stomach hurts. Please give me medicine.
examples.explanation: Simple and direct communication is best at the pharmacy or hospital.

examples.targetText: きのう、でんしゃで かばんを ぬすまれて しまいました。
examples.transliteration: Kinou, densha de kaban wo nusumarete shimaimashita.
examples.baseTranslation: Yesterday, my bag was unfortunately stolen on the train.
examples.explanation: Combines the passive voice (nusumareru) with the regret form (shimaimashita) for a complete police report.

importantRules.ruleText: Use 'ga' to mark the body part that hurts (Atama GA itai).
importantRules.exceptionNotes: Do not use 'wa', as you are pinpointing the specific source of pain.

importantRules.ruleText: To say 'I lost it', use 'nakushimashita'. To say 'It was stolen', use 'nusumaremashita'.
importantRules.exceptionNotes: Be careful not to mix these up at the police station!

cultureContext.contextText: If you lose something in Japan, always go to the nearest Kouban (police box). The recovery rate for lost wallets and phones in Japan is incredibly high.
dosList: Say 'Tasukete!' (Help!) if you are in immediate physical danger.
dontsList: Do not panic if the police ask you to fill out a long form (Ishitubutsu-todoke) for a lost item. It is standard procedure.

summary: Use 'ga itai' for pain, '~te shimaimashita' for unfortunate accidents, and the passive voice ('nusumaremashita') for crimes.

checkpointQuestions.expectedAnswer: おなかが いたいです (Onaka ga itai desu)

checkpointQuestions.questionText: What does '~te shimaimashita' add to a sentence?
checkpointQuestions.expectedAnswer: The feeling that something happened unfortunately or accidentally.
さいふを ぬすまれました (Saifu wo nusumaremashita)

keyTakeaways: Symptoms: Body Part + ga + itai desu.
Victim of crime: Object + wo + nusumaremashita (Passive).
adminCorrectAnswerSet.conceptSummary: Communicating clearly during stressful emergency situations.

checkpointAnswers.q1: おなかが いたいです
checkpointAnswers.q3: さいふを ぬすまれました

adminCorrectAnswerSet.expectedUnderstanding: Learner must memorize the emergency vocabulary for their own safety.

feedbackRules.onPass: Great job! You are now prepared to handle emergencies in Japan.
feedbackRules.onFail: Review the medical vocabulary. 'Itai' means painful.
feedbackRules.onPartial: Good start. Make sure you practice the difference between losing something (nakusu) and having it stolen (nusumareru).

audioAssets.audioTranscript: あたまが いたいです。ねつが あります。せきが でます。かぜを ひきました。さいふを なくして しまいました。パスポートを ぬすまれました。きゅうきゅうしゃを よんで ください！

commonMistakes: Saying 'Atama wa itai' instead of 'Atama ga itai'.
